title: Jeremiah 25
subtitle: Seventy Years and the Cup of Wrath

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Seventy Years Prophecy

sections.header: Verses 1-14 -- Seventy Years of Babylonian Exile

heb.paragraph: God announces that the exile will last seventy years (v.11). This number is both literal and typological: the exile from 605 to 538 BC is approximately 67 years; from the temple's destruction (586) to its rebuilding (516) is exactly 70. The number also represents a complete generation -- the fullness of judgment. Daniel later takes this prophecy as the basis for his prayer of national repentance (Dan 9:2).

refs.note: Daniel reads Jeremiah's seventy-year prophecy and launches his great prayer of repentance -- one prophetic text generating another.
The Chronicler interprets the seventy years as Sabbath rest for the land (cf. Lev 26:34-35) -- the years of rest the land was denied during centuries of disobedience.
Cyrus's decree fulfills the seventy-year prophecy: "The LORD moved the heart of Cyrus king of Persia."

notes.note: Twenty-three years of prophetic ministry -- "again and again I have spoken to you, but you have not listened." The patience of God has a limit; the twenty-three years demonstrate extraordinary divine forbearance before irrevocable judgment.
Nebuchadnezzar is called "my servant" -- a stunning title for a pagan king. God uses imperial power to accomplish covenant purposes. The king of Babylon is an instrument, not an autonomous agent.

The seventy years: after completion, God will "punish the king of Babylon and his nation." The instrument of judgment becomes the object of judgment. No earthly power is exempt from divine accountability.

Calvin marvels at the title "my servant" for Nebuchadnezzar: "God employs even those who do not know him. The pagan king serves God's purpose unknowingly -- which makes divine sovereignty all the more astonishing."
Calvin reads the seventy years as a pastoral mercy: "God sets a limit to judgment. The exile is not eternal but measured. Even in wrath, God remembers mercy."
The "fourth year of Jehoiakim" (605 BC) coincides with the first year of Nebuchadnezzar and the Battle of Carchemish -- the geopolitical watershed that made Babylon the dominant world power.
The "seventy years" has been interpreted variously: a round number for a lifetime (Ps 90:10), the literal span 605-538 BC, or the temple gap 586-516 BC. All three readings have merit.

Lundbom identifies chapter 25 as the structural hinge of the entire book: it summarizes the first half (judgment oracles, chs. 1-24) and introduces the second half (oracles against the nations, chs. 46-51). The seventy-year prophecy is the temporal framework for both.

Brueggemann reads "my servant Nebuchadnezzar" as "the most astonishing title in prophetic literature." God claims the imperial aggressor as his agent. This subverts both Babylonian self-understanding and Judean nationalism.

hist.context: Chapter 25 is dated to "the fourth year of Jehoiakim" (605 BC) -- the year of the Battle of Carchemish when Babylon replaced Egypt as the superpower. This oracle summarizes twenty-three years of Jeremiah's rejected ministry (627-605 BC) and announces the definitive judgment: seventy years of Babylonian domination. But the chapter also looks beyond exile: Babylon itself will be punished (v.12), establishing the pattern that God's instruments of judgment are themselves judged.

sections.header: Verses 15-38 -- The Cup of God's Wrath

heb.paragraph: God gives Jeremiah "this cup filled with the wine of my wrath" (kos hayyin hahamema, v.15) to make all nations drink. The cup metaphor for divine judgment appears throughout Scripture (Ps 75:8, Isa 51:17, Rev 14:10). The imagery is of forced intoxication -- the nations will "drink and stagger and go mad" (v.16). The cup passes to twenty-three nations, beginning with Jerusalem and ending with "the king of Sheshak" -- a cipher for Babylon.

refs.note: "He too will drink the wine of God's fury" -- Revelation draws directly on Jeremiah's cup-of-wrath imagery for eschatological judgment.
Jesus prays: "Take this cup from me" -- the Gethsemane prayer echoes the wrath-cup tradition, with Christ drinking the cup that the nations deserved.
"Judgment begins with God's household" -- the same principle Jeremiah articulates in v.29.

notes.note: The cup of wrath is both metaphor and prophetic sign-act. Jeremiah does not literally carry a cup to each nation but symbolically "makes them drink" through prophetic proclamation.

"Sheshak" is an atbash cipher for Babylon -- the last letter of the Hebrew alphabet replaces the first, etc. The encoding may reflect the danger of openly naming Babylon as a target of divine judgment.
"If the city that bears my Name must drink it..." -- Jerusalem goes first. If God judges his own covenant people, no nation on earth can expect exemption.
God roars from on high like a lion; he brings charges against all nations. The courtroom and battlefield merge: God is both judge and warrior.

Calvin develops the principle: "If God does not spare his own people, whom he loves, how much less will he spare those who have no covenant with him? The severity of grace is the measure of all other judgment."

The atbash cipher "Sheshak" (shin-shin-kaf) encodes "Babel" (bet-bet-lamed) by substituting each letter with its reverse-alphabet equivalent. The same technique appears in Jer 51:1 ("Leb Kamai" = "Chaldea").
"Weep and wail, you shepherds" -- the "shepherds" are kings and rulers, continuing the pastoral metaphor from chapters 22-23.

Lundbom reads the cup oracle as "the universal extension of Jeremiah's mandate." The prophet commissioned "over nations and kingdoms" (1:10) now exercises that authority across the entire known world.

Brueggemann identifies a "leveling theology" -- every nation, from covenant people to distant empire, stands equally under divine judgment. No special status exempts any people from accountability. Jerusalem and Babylon drink from the same cup.

hist.context: The cup-of-wrath oracle is one of the most expansive judgment visions in the Hebrew Bible. Beginning with Jerusalem and spreading outward to Egypt, Uz, Philistia, Edom, Moab, Ammon, Tyre, Sidon, Arabia, and finally Babylon (encoded as "Sheshak"), the vision portrays universal judgment. "If the city that bears my Name must drink it, should you go unpunished?" (v.29). Judgment begins with God's own house and extends to every nation.

rows.label: vv.8-14
rows.text: Seventy Years: Judgment and Its Limit

rows.label: vv.15-29
rows.text: The Cup of Wrath for All Nations

lit.note: Chapter 25 is the structural hinge of the book: it summarizes the judgment oracles (chs. 1-24), announces the seventy-year timeframe, and extends judgment universally through the cup of wrath.

themes.note: Judgment peaks at 10 -- the cup of wrath and universal scope. Sovereignty at 9: Nebuchadnezzar as God's "servant." Mission at 6: the prophet's mandate extends to all nations.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Seventy Years and the Cup of Wrath

translations.text: “But you did not listen to me,” declares the Lord, “and you have aroused my anger with what your hands have made, and you have brought harm to yourselves.”
Yet you have not listened to me, declares the LORD, that you might provoke me to anger with the work of your hands to your own harm.

I will summon all the peoples of the north and my servant Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon,” declares the Lord, “and I will bring them against this land and its inhabitants and against all the surrounding nations. I will completely destroy them and make them an object of horror and scorn, and an everlasting ruin.

behold, I will send for all the tribes of the north, declares the LORD, and for Nebuchadnezzar the king of Babylon, my servant, and I will bring them against this land and its inhabitants, and against all these surrounding nations. I will devote them to destruction, and make them a horror, a hissing, and an everlasting desolation.

debate: The "seventy years" has been interpreted variously: a round number for a lifetime (Ps 90:10), the literal span 605-538 BC, or the temple gap 586-516 B…
Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Seventy Years and the Cup of Wrath within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.text: "Judgment begins with God's household" -- the same principle Jeremiah articulates in v.29.

coaching.tip: 'Seventy years' (v. 11) becomes one of the Old Testament's most consequential numbers. Daniel reads this passage in exile and prays for its fulfillment (Daniel 9:2). Zechariah references it (1:12). The number becomes a hinge between two prophets and two eras. Jeremiah writes a sentence; Daniel prays it into reality. Scripture interpreting Scripture is already happening within the Old Testament itself.

The 'cup of God's wrath' (v. 15) passed from nation to nation — every kingdom must drink. Jesus uses this exact imagery in Gethsemane: 'Take this cup from me' (Mark 14:36). The cup Jeremiah describes as passing to the nations, Jesus takes upon himself. The trajectory from Jeremiah 25 to Gethsemane shows what atonement costs: someone drinks the cup so the nations don't have to.
